title: WSJ:  Why the Memory Crunch Is Almost Impossible to Solve
gist: A reposted WSJ report: AI demand has triggered a structural memory-chip shortage that is hard to fix because only Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron dominate DRAM and new fabs take years; AI buyers with the deepest pockets are outbidding consumer-tech firms (forcing Apple price hikes), the cautious oligopoly is wary of another glut despite record margins (Micron ~80% gross margin), and the China relief valve (CXMT, YMTC) is blocked by national-security rules.

insights.claim: Only Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron dominate DRAM and are devoting much of their capacity to fast-growing AI demand, squeezing consumer-tech companies, with new fabs taking years to build.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A three-player DRAM oligopoly prioritizing AI is the supply-side root of the crunch; pricing power sits with the incumbents through the AI cycle.
insights.quote: Three industry titans—Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix and Micron Technology—dominate the business of memory chips, also called DRAM. They are using much of their capacity to serve the fast-growing artificial-intelligence industry, squeezing consumer-technology companies.
insights.locator: Only a handful of companies make memory and storage chips...

insights.claim: Burned by past boom-bust cycles, the big memory producers (and NAND makers including Kioxia and Sandisk) are keeping new-plant spending relatively conservative despite record gross margins — Micron's reaching 80%.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Capacity discipline at record margins means the shortage persists and margins stay elevated; bullish for memory-maker earnings, bearish for memory buyers.
insights.quote: gross profit margins that have soared to records: in Micron’s case, 80%.

insights.claim: US memory-fab relief is years away: the first new Micron Idaho facility won't open until mid-next year and its New York (Clay/Syracuse) plant won't start production until 2030.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Concrete timeline showing no near-term domestic supply relief; the crunch is structurally locked in for several years regardless of CHIPS Act subsidies.
insights.quote: the first of the new Idaho facilities won’t open until the middle of next year, and the New York plant won’t start production until 2030.
insights.locator: U.S. lawmakers have approved tens of billions of dollars...

insights.claim: AI demand took off after the 2023 downturn (when SK Hynix lost billions and Micron cut 15% of staff), stoking demand for AI-suited memory plus large quantities of traditional memory for AI data centers; SK Hynix is now valued above $1 trillion alongside Samsung and Micron.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The 2023-to-now swing from layoffs to trillion-dollar valuations shows the cycle's violence; positioning into memory is a leveraged AI-capex play.
insights.quote: As recently as 2023, South Korea’s SK Hynix—now valued at more than $1 trillion alongside Samsung and Micron—was losing billions of dollars and cutting production during an industry downturn.

insights.claim: AI companies with the deepest pockets are outbidding everyone else for memory, forcing the rest of the market (e.g. Apple raising prices) to pay up.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Memory-cost inflation is passing through to consumer-device prices; a margin/pricing risk for consumer hardware OEMs across the cycle.
insights.quote: “The AI companies have the deepest pockets in the economy and they’re outbidding everyone else for memory,” said Jim Secreto
insights.locator: “The AI companies have the deepest pockets...

insights.claim: China is the closest thing to a quick fix but only a partial one: Yangtze Memory (YMTC) is building three new NAND fabs to more than double capacity by end-2027, and CXMT is seeking a ~$4 billion Shanghai IPO with Q1 2026 revenue up more than 700% YoY, though both still trail the three leaders.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Chinese memory makers are scaling fast and seeking global clients; their capacity additions are the key swing factor for whether the crunch eases by 2027.
insights.quote: It said its revenue rose by more than 700% year-over-year in the first quarter of 2026, though it acknowledged that its products still trail those of the three industry leaders.

insights.claim: US national-security rules make it hard for American firms to work with Chinese memory makers, and Micron has been lobbying for even stronger restrictions backed by bipartisan legislation, protecting Micron and allied Korean/Japanese suppliers.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Policy actively blocks the China relief valve to protect Micron's market position; the regulatory bind keeps prices high and is a key risk/lobbying battleground.
insights.quote: Micron, an Apple supplier, has been lobbying for even stronger restrictions on U.S. partnerships with Chinese firms deemed security risks
insights.locator: U.S. national-security rules make it hard...

insights.claim: The shortage has grown bad enough that big consumer-tech firms have asked officials to loosen China restrictions, Apple CEO Tim Cook said 'everything needs to be on the table,' and PC makers including HP are in talks to use CXMT chips in Asia-bound products.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Consumer-tech lobbying to loosen China rules signals real pain; watch policy shifts and CXMT design-ins (HP) as the relief-valve indicator.
insights.quote: Apple Chief Executive Tim Cook said in a Wall Street Journal interview that “everything needs to be on the table” regarding policy choices.
insights.locator: Yet the memory-chip shortage has gotten bad enough...
